item.title: decision tree
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(
ewcommand\dif[1]{\:	extrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{ceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter loor{\lfloor}{floor}\) \(
ewcommand{\ihat}{\hat{	extbf{i}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\jhat}{\hat{	extbf{j}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) a decision tree represents a function that takes as input a vector of attribute values and returns a “decision”–a single output value. the input and output values can be discrete or continuous for a wide variety of problems, the decision tree yields a nice, concise result. but some functions cannot be represented concisely, for example, the majority function, which returns true if and only if more than half of the inputs are true, requires an exponentially large decision tree.

item.title: select algorithm
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(
ewcommand\dif[1]{\:	extrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{ceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter loor{\lfloor}{floor}\) \(
ewcommand{\ihat}{\hat{	extbf{i}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\jhat}{\hat{	extbf{j}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) an algorithm that would help us reduce the time complexity of many problems input: an array \(A\) of \(n\) numbers and a normal number \(k\) such that \(1 \le k \le n\) output: the element in \(A\) whose rank is \(k\), i.e. the \(k\)’th smallest number in \(A\) example: for the input \(k=3,A=\{13,15,16,14,12,18,17,11\}\), the output is 13 a naive approach: the correctness of this algorithm trivially follows from the definition of the given problem we propose a more afficient algorithm that we’ll call \(	extsc{Select}\) which solves the problem in linear time: given an array \(A\) of \(n\) different numbers, and a number \(k\), \(1 \le k \le n\), let \(e_k\) be a number such that \(ank(e_k,A)=k\) assume that the partition algorithm was applied to \(A\) and we got the subarrays \(A_l,A_r\) of sizes \(m,n-m\) if \(k \le m\) then \(e_k\) is in \(A_l\) and \(ank(e_k,A_l)=k\) if \(k>m\) then \(e_k\) is in \(A_r\) and \(ank(e_k,A_r)=k-m\) for example, given the array \(A=\{3,8,-2,13,11,6,9,2,14,7\},pivot=8,k=6\) we’d get \(A_l=\{3,-2,6,2,7\},A_r=\{8,13,11,9,14\}\) in \(A_l\) there are the 5 smallest numbers from \(A\), the sixth smallest number in A, 8, is the minimal number in \(A_r\) the correctness of the algorithm follows trivially from the lemma we are sacrificing linear time finding a good pivot so that the partition algorithm’s worst-case performance doesnt reach \(\Theta(n^2)\), which may happen when the size of one of the partitions happens to be 1 repeatedly this algorithm runs in linear time a company has \(n\) employees, their salaries differ and are given in an array \(A\), it wants to give its employees bonuses (it wants to spend as much as it can, given a limited budget), a bonus is the same amount as ones salary, given \(k\), the sum of bonuses is less or equal to a given number \(k\), solve the problem in \(\Theta(n)\) the recurrence formula for this algorithm is \(T(n)=Cn+T\left( rac{n}{2}ight)\) which simplifies to \(T(n) \in \Theta(n)\)

ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) dynamic programming amounts to breaking down an optimization problem into simpler sub-problems, and storing the solution to each sub-problem so that each sub-problem is only solved once. to be honest, this definition may not make total sense until you see an example of a sub-problem given the following problem: input: an array of positive numbers of size \(n\) output: a subarray (non-consecutive) whose sum is maximal that doesnt contain two consecutive elements example: input \(3,{ f 12},6\) output 12 example: input \({ f 5},4,{ f 9}\) output 14 example: input \({ f 13},3,5,{ f 9}\) output 22 example: input \({ f 12},5,6,{ f 17},9\) output 29 we can stem the following 2 lemmas: the optimal solution (i.

item.title: recursion tree
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(
ewcommand\dif[1]{\:	extrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{ceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter loor{\lfloor}{floor}\) \(
ewcommand{\ihat}{\hat{	extbf{i}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\jhat}{\hat{	extbf{j}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) consider the following \[ T(n) = T\left( rac{n}{3}ight) + T\left( rac{2n}{3}ight) + n \] a note to keep in mind is that the sum of all the nodes of the tree must always be equal to \(T(n)\) with that in mind, the first step would be: if we sum all the nodes we can see that indeed \(T(n) = T\left(\dfrac{2n}{3}ight) + T\left(\dfrac{n}{3}ight) + n\) for the next step we need to write \(T\left(\dfrac{2n}{3}ight)\) and \(T\left(\dfrac{n}{3}ight)\) in terms of time complexity for smaller values of \(n\) so we can know what the next row of nodes would be  egin{align*} T\left( rac{2n}{3}ight) &= T\left( rac{2 \cdot  rac{2n}{3}}{3}ight) + T\left( rac{ rac{2n}{3}}{3}ight) +  rac{2n}{3} = T\left( rac{4n}{9}ight) + T\left( rac{2n}{9}ight) +  rac{2n}{3}\ T\left( rac{n}{3}ight) &= T\left( rac{2 \cdot  rac{n}{3}}{3}ight) + T\left( rac{ rac{n}{3}}{3}ight) +  rac{n}{3} = T\left( rac{2n}{9}ight) + T\left( rac{n}{9}ight) +  rac{n}{3} \end{align*}

item.title: closed form
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(

ewcommand{\jhat}{\hat{	extbf{j}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) to solve a , we find a closed form for it, a closed form for \(T(n)\) is an equation that defines \(T(n)\) using an expression that does not involve \(T\) there is no single method that works for all, we check for patterns and use substitution to get a better understanding, we look at examples consider the following function that returns the factorial of a given number def fac(n): if n == 1: return 1 else: return n * fac(n-1) print([fac(i) for i in range(1,10)]) for fac(n), how many times is fac called?

item.title: recurrence relation
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(

ewcommand{\jhat}{\hat{	extbf{j}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) to analyze the time complexity of recursive functions we use a mathematical concept called recurrence relation a recurrence defines \(T(n)\) in terms of \(T\) for smaller values \(T(n) = T(n-1) + 1\), here \(T(n)\) is defined in terms of \(T(n-1)\)

item.title: algorithm correctness
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(
ewcommand\dif[1]{\:	extrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{ceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter loor{\lfloor}{floor}\) \(
ewcommand{\ihat}{\hat{	extbf{i}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\jhat}{\hat{	extbf{j}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) an algorithm is totally correct if it receives valid input, terminates, and always returns the correct output the only way to prove the correctness of an algorithm over all possible inputs is by reasoning formally or mathematically about it. generally, to prove an algorithm is correct we prove two things the algorithm terminates the algorithm returns the correct value for every input we propose a statement that describes what the algorithm takes as input and what its supposed to output and then prove it 1 proving algorithm correctness by induction here we’ll be using mathematical induction for each input that is an array of numbers A, the algorithm \(	extsc{Sum-Array}\) computes the sum of the numbers proof the algorithm terminates: the algorithm consists of a for loop that is bound by n, so it follows trivially that it must terminate proof of correctness by perfect induction on the length of the input array we propose the following loop invariant for the loop \[ sum = \sum_{j=1}^{i} A[j] \] in this case, proving the proposed loop invariant would prove the algorithm correct for \(i=1\), which is the base case, the algorithm returns A[1] which would be the only element in the array, so its obvious that its the correct output the induction hypothesis, we assume after i iterations in the loop that \(sum = \sum_{j=1}^{i} A[j]\) the induction step, during the \(i+1\)’th iteration of the loop, we add \(A[i+1]\) to the variable sum, and so it follows from this and from the induction hypothesis that: \[ sum = \sum_{j=1}^{i} A[j] + A[i+1] = \sum_{j=1}^{i+1} A[j] \] the loop runs n times, by substituting n in the loop invariant, we’d get \[ sum = \sum_{j=1}^{n} A[j] \] which means that after the loop is done, the variable sum would contain the sum of the elements in the array, which is what the function returns, which is the correct output QED 2 correctness of recursive algorithms the pattern for proofs of recursive algorithms is as follows: base step of the recursion, we have to manually show that for each of the edge cases the output is correct induction hypothesis, we assume that in each recurring call the output is correct proof, we show that the output is correct for the main function call consider the following function proof that the algorithm terminates: assume that \(	extsc{Recursive-Sum}\) runs on an array A, on each recurring call, the size of the input array A is halved, its obvious that at the end of every sequence of calls the size of the input array would arrive to 1 which is the edge case that would cause the function to terminate proof of correctness: we prove this by induction on the number of elements in the array base case: for \(|A|=1\) which is the edge case, the algorithm returns \(A[1]\), the only element in \(A\), its obvious that the output is correct induction hypothesis: we use perfect induction on the size of the input, we assume that the algorithm correctly computes the sum of the elements of any array whose size is smaller than n induction step: let A be an arbitrary array of size n, the call to \(	extsc{Recursive-Sum}(A)\) executes the calls \(	extsc{Recursive-Sum}(A_l)\) and \(	extsc{Recursive-Sum}(A_r)\), the size of the arrays \(A_l\) and \(A_r\) is smaller than \(n\), so according to the previous step we know those two recurring calls do return the correct sum of the corresponding subarrays, and since we are returning the sum of those sums we know for sure we are returning the correct sum

item.title: algorithms homework 3
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(
ewcommand\dif[1]{\:	extrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{ceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter loor{\lfloor}{floor}\) \(
ewcommand{\ihat}{\hat{	extbf{i}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\jhat}{\hat{	extbf{j}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) homework on recursion please do provide feedback on mistakes you find, if any write a recursive algorithm that finds the 2 biggest numbers in a given array that halves the array on each recursive call python version of the algorithm: def biggest_two(arr): if len(arr) == 1: return arr[0], -1000 if len(arr) == 2: return arr[0], arr[1] left_1, left_2 = biggest_two(arr[0:len(arr)//2]) right_1, right_2 = biggest_two(arr[len(arr)//2:len(arr)]) biggest_1 = max({left_1, left_2, right_1, right_2}) biggest_2 = max({left_1, left_2, right_1, right_2} - {biggest_1}) return biggest_1, biggest_2 print(biggest_two([10, 20, 30, 35, 2, 17])) (35, 30) find the time complexity of the algorithm we consider the \(T(n)=2T\left( rac{n}{2}ight) + C\):  egin{align*} T(n) &= 2T\left( rac{n}{2}ight) + C\ T\left( rac{n}{2}ight) &= 2T\left( rac{n}{4}ight) + C\ T\left( rac{n}{4}ight) &= 2T\left( rac{n}{8}ight) + C\ T\left( rac{n}{2^{\lg n}}ight) &= 2T\left( rac{n}{2^{\lg n+1}}ight) + C\ T(n) &= 2\left(2T\left( rac{n}{4}ight) + Cight) + C = 4T\left( rac{n}{4}ight) + 3C\ &= 4\left(2T\left( rac{n}{8}ight)+Cight)+3C = 8T\left( rac{n}{8}ight)+7C\ &= 8\left(2T\left( rac{n}{16}ight)+Cight)+7C = 16T\left( rac{n}{16}ight)+15C\ &\vdots\ &= 2^{\lg n}T\left( rac{n}{2^{\lg n}}ight) + \sum_{i=0}^{\lg n} 2^{i}C\ &= 2^{\lg n}C_1 + \sum_{i=0}^{\lg n} 2^{i}C\ &= 2^{\lg n}C_1 +  rac{1-2^{\lg(n)+1}}{-1}C\ &= 2^{\lg n}C_1-C+2^{\lg(n)+1}\ &\Rightarrow \Theta\left(2^{\lg n}ight) = \Theta(n) \end{align*}

ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) probability is a function, denoted by \(P\) that describes the probability for a specific incident to occur the probability of an impossible event is zero; the probability of a certain event is one. therefore, for any event \(A\), the range of possible probabilities is \(0 \le P(A) \le 1\) for \(S\) the sample space of all possibilities, \(P(S)=1\).

ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) AVL sort consists of inserting the given array into an AVL tree element by element and then converting the tree into an array using inorder traversal the time complexity of this algorithm is \(\Theta(n\log n)\)

item.title: comparison sort
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(
ewcommand\dif[1]{\:	extrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{ceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter loor{\lfloor}{floor}\) \(
ewcommand{\ihat}{\hat{	extbf{i}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\jhat}{\hat{	extbf{j}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) a comparison sort is a type of sorting algorithm that only reads the list elements through a single abstract comparison operation (often a “less than or equal to” operator or a three-way comparison) that determines which of two elements should occur first in the final sorted list. any comparison-sort based algorithm has a time complexity that is bound by \(\Omega(n\log n)\) a vector A of size n is defined to be a “hill” iff for every \(i \le i < j \le m\) we know \(A[i] \le A[j]\) and for every \(m \le i < j \le n\) we know \(A[i] \geq A[j]\) where \(m\) is a constant and \(i,j\) are variable indicies prove that any comparison-based algorithm that turns a vector into a hill has to perform \(\Omega(n\log n)\) operations we assume in contradiction that there exists a comparison-based algorithm that turns a vector into a hill whose time complexity is less than \(\Omega(n\log n)\) consider the following function: each line from 3 to 6 runs in \(\Theta(n)\) time, except the first which we know by the assumption runs in less than \(\Theta(n\log n)\), so the whole function runs in less than \(\Theta(n\log n)\) time this is a comparison-based function that sorts a given array in less than \(\Theta(n\log n)\) time, which contradicts with our theorem that no sorting function can have a lower time complexity than \(\Theta(n\log n)\) we arrived at a contradiction and so it has to be true that this function is bound from below by \(\Theta(n\log n)\), i.

item.title: heap sort
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(
ewcommand\dif[1]{\:	extrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{ceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter loor{\lfloor}{floor}\) \(
ewcommand{\ihat}{\hat{	extbf{i}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\jhat}{\hat{	extbf{j}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) heap sort consists of transforming the given array into a heap array A, initiating heap-size to size(A) then applying the following steps recursively: replace the root of the heap A[0] with A[heap-size - 1] bubble down the newly replaced root (A[0]) decrement heap-size, return to step 1 if heap-size > 1 the time complexity of this algorithm is \(\Theta(n\log n)\) on the third step when decrementing heap-size we arent deleting the last element, we are simply decreasing the size of the part of the list that we’re operating on because the part at the end contains elements that are sorted

item.title: insertion sort
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(
ewcommand\dif[1]{\:	extrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{ceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter loor{\lfloor}{floor}\) \(
ewcommand{\ihat}{\hat{	extbf{i}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\jhat}{\hat{	extbf{j}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) iterate from arr[1] to arr[n] compare the current element to its predecessor if the current element is smaller than its predecessor, drag it backwards until its bigger than its predecessor time complexity is \(O(n^2)\) template <typename T> void insertion_sort(T arr[], int len) { for (int i = 1; i < len; ++i) { int idx = i; while (idx > 0 && arr[idx] < arr[idx-1]) { T tmp = arr[idx]; arr[idx] = arr[idx-1]; arr[idx-1] = tmp; idx--; } } }

item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(

ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) QuickSort is a divide-and-conquer sorting algorithm, it picks an element as pivot and partitions the given array around the pivot, there are different versions of quicksort that pick pivots in different ways: first element as pivot (implemented here) last element as pivot a random element as pivot median as pivot the first subarray we use as a “partition” is the array itself, where given a pivot, put the pivot at its correct position in the sorted array by putting all the smaller elements before it, and all the greater elements after it.

item.title: bubble sort
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(

ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) this algorithm repeatedly steps through the input list element by element, comparing the current element with the one after it, swapping their values if needed. these passes through the list are repeated until no swaps had to be performed during a pass, meaning that the list has become fully sorted. the algorithm, which is a comparison sort, is named for the way the larger elements “bubble” up to the top of the list.

item.title: greedy algorithm
item.description: the method of greediness proposes that in each step in an algorithm, where we are presented with multiple choices, the choice with the biggest instantaneous gain is picked using this method we can write algorithm that, while relatively simple, arent usually optimal input: an array A of positive numbers output: a subarray (non-consecutive) whose sum is maximal which doesnt contain two consecutive elements the algorithm \(\textsc{Greedy1}(A)\) picks the biggest number in the input array, adds it to the total sum, and discards the two neighboring elements but is this algorithm correct?

item.title: selection sort
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(

ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) selection sort maintains two subarrays in a given array, one subarray that is sorted and the other unsorted, it sorts a given array by repeatedly finding the minimum element (considering ascending order) from the unsorted part and putting it at the beginning in every iteration of selection sort, the minimum element (considering ascending order) from the unsorted subarray is picked and moved to the sorted subarray.

item.title: sorting algorithms
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(

ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) 1 comparison sort a comparison sort is a type of sorting algorithm that only reads the list elements through a single abstract comparison operation (often a “less than or equal to” operator or a three-way comparison) that determines which of two elements should occur first in the final sorted list. any comparison-sort based algorithm has a time complexity that is bound by \(\Omega(n\log n)\) a vector A of size n is defined to be a “hill” iff for every \(i \le i < j \le m\) we know \(A[i] \le A[j]\) and for every \(m \le i < j \le n\) we know \(A[i] \geq A[j]\) where \(m\) is a constant and \(i,j\) are variable indicies prove that any comparison-based algorithm that turns a vector into a hill has to perform \(\Omega(n\log n)\) operations we assume in contradiction that there exists a comparison-based algorithm that turns a vector into a hill whose time complexity is less than \(\Omega(n\log n)\) consider the following function: each line from 3 to 6 runs in \(\Theta(n)\) time, except the first which we know by the assumption runs in less than \(\Theta(n\log n)\), so the whole function runs in less than \(\Theta(n\log n)\) time this is a comparison-based function that sorts a given array in less than \(\Theta(n\log n)\) time, which contradicts with our theorem that no sorting function can have a lower time complexity than \(\Theta(n\log n)\) we arrived at a contradiction and so it has to be true that this function is bound from below by \(\Theta(n\log n)\), i.

ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) its important to read the exercises provided on every subject because i explain important things with exercises headlines without content act as placeholders for now. 1 factoring formulas  egin{align} a^2-b^2 &= (a-b)(a+b)\ (a+b)^2 &= a^2+2ab+b^2\ (a-b)^2 &= a^2-2ab+b^2\ ax^2+bx+c &= a(x-x_1)(x-x_2)\ a^3-b^3 &= (a-b)(a^2+b^2+ab)\ a^3+b^3 &= (a+b)(a^2-ab+b^2)\ (a-b)^3 &= a^3-b^3-3ab(a-b)\ (a+b)^3 &= a^3+b^3+3ab(a+b)\ (a+b+c)^3 &= a^3+b^3+c^3+3(a+b)(b+c)(c+a)\  rac{1}{k(k+1)} &=  rac1k -  rac{1}{k+1} \end{align}

ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) events A and B are disjoint if they cannot occur at the same time, \(P(A \cap B) = 0\) given A,B are disjoint events, \[ P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) \], and vice versa generally, \(P(A \cup B) = P(A)+P(B)-P(A \cap B)\) so \(P(A \cap B)=\varnothing\) iff \(P(A \cup B) = P(A)+P(B)\)

item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(

ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) a probability distribution the probability for a success on the \(k\)’th attempt with success probability \(p\) is \[ P(k) = {(1-p)}^{k-1}p \] because we have \(k-1\) failures which gives us \({(1-p)}^{k-1}\), and a single success on the last attempt so we multiply by \(p\) a random variable has geometric distributivity if it receives the values \(1,2,\dots\) with probabilities \(P(X=k)\) the symbol of the geometric distribution is \(X \sim G(p)\) the expected number of trials for a success is \(E[k] =  rac{1}{p}\) variance is \(\mathrm{Var}= rac{1-p}{p^2}\) formula of “at most” in a geometric distribution is \(P(x \le k)=1-(1-p)^k\) formula of “at least” \(P(x \geq k)=(1-p)^{k-1}\)

item.title: variance
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(

ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) let \(X\) be a random variable whose expected value is finite \(\mu=\mathrm{E}(X)\), the variance of \(X\), denoted by \(\mathrm{Var}(X)\), is defined as \[ \mathrm{Var}(X) = \mathrm{E}\left(\left(X-\muight)^2ight) \] let \(X\) be a random variable whose distribution law is \[ x =  egin{cases} -1 & 1/4\ 0 & 1/4\ 1 & 1/2 \end{cases} \] we find the variance:  egin{gather*} \mu = \mathrm{E}(X) = -1 \cdot  rac{1}{4} + 0 \cdot  rac{1}{4} + 1 \cdot  rac{1}{2} =  rac{1}{4}\ \mathrm{Var}(X) = \mathrm{E}\left(\left(X- rac{1}{4}ight)^2ight) = \left(-1- rac{1}{4}ight)^2 \cdot  rac{1}{4} + \left(0- rac{1}{4}ight)^2 \cdot  rac{1}{4} + \left(1- rac{1}{4}ight) \cdot  rac{1}{2} =  rac{11}{16} \end{gather*}

\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(

ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) a probability distribution out of \(n\) different items of which \(m\) are of a specific type and \(n-m\) are of another type, we pick \(k\) items without repetition, the probability that from \(k\) items \(r\) are of the first type and \(k-r\) are of the second type is: \[ P_n(k) =  rac{ inom{m}{r}  inom{n-m}{k-r}}{ inom{n}{k}} \] the symbol of the hypergeometric distribution is \(X \sim H(k,m,n)\) expected value is \(\mathrm{E}(x)=n \cdot  rac{R}{N}\) variance is \(\mathrm{Var}(x)= rac{k \cdot m \cdot (n-m) \cdot (n-k)}{n^2 \cdot (n-1)}\)

item.title: binomial distribution
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(

ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) a probability distribution the probability of a specific incident to occur \(k\) times out of \(n\) times is a combination, so: \[ P(k) =  inom{n}{k}p^k{(1-p)}^{n-k} \] such that \(k=0,1,\dots,n\), \(0 \le P \le 1\) a random variable is binomially distributive with the parameters \(n,p\) and is written as \(X \sim B(n,p)\) if \(X\) receives the values \(0,1,\dots,n\) with the probabilities \(P(X=k)\) (the formula above) the symbol of the binomial distribution is \(X \sim B(n,p)\) expected value is \(E(x)=np\) variance is \(np(1-p)\) 1 bernoulli trial Independent repeated trials of an experiment with exactly two possible outcomes are called Bernoulli trials.

ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) a random variable is a discrete random variable iff it receives a finite or infinite series \(X_1,X_2,\dots\) of the values of the function \(P_x\) that is defined by \(P_x(X_i)=P(X=X_i)\) where \(i=1,2,\dots\) which is called the probability function of the random variable \(X\) \[ 0 \le P_x(X_i) \le 1 \] \[ \sum_{i} P_x(X_i) = 1 \] if \(X\) is a discrete random variable then the list of values that it could receive and their corresponding probabilities are called the distribution law or the probability law of said variable

item.title: random variable
item.description: a random variable X is a function \(X(\Omega)\) from the sample space to the real line (\(\mathbb{R}\)), such that every event in the sample space corresponds to a real number. \(P_i\) is the probability of receiving the value \(X_i\) and \(P(X)\) is the probability function there are two types of random variables discrete random variable continuous random variable we roll a symmetric coin 3 times \[ \Omega = \{(h,h,h),(h,h,t),(h,t,h),(t,h,h),(t,h,t),(h,t,t),(t,t,h),(t,t,t)\} \] we define \(X\) as the number of times that “heads” was received \(X\) is a random variable with 4 possible values, \(X(\Omega)=0,1,2,3\), \(P(X=0)=\frac{1}{8},P(X=1)=\frac{3}{8},P(X=2)=\frac{3}{8},P(X=3)=\frac{1}{8}\) let \(f_i\) be the frequency of the value \(x_i\), we get \(\sum_{i}^{k} f_i = n\), so \(\overline{x} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} x_if_i}{n}\) let \(p_i=\frac{f_i}{n}\) be the probability to get the value \(x_i\), so \(\sum_{i}^{k} p_i=1\), we get \(\overline{x}=\frac{\sum_{1}^{k} x_if_i}{n}=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{k} x_if_i=\sum_{i=1}^{k} x_i \cdot \frac{f_i}{n}=\sum_{i=1}^{k} x_ip_i\) \[ \overline{x} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} x_ip_i \] let \(x_1,x_2,\dots,x_k\) be arbitrary values and \(f_1,f_2,\dots,f_k\) their frequencies the variances of \(x_1,x_2,\dots,x_k\) is the measure of their scattering around the average \[ S^2 = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{k} (x_i-\overline{x})^2 f_i \] we build off from the previous lemmas: \begin{align*} S^2 &= \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{k} (x_i-\overline{x})^2 f_i\\ &= \sum_{i=1}^{k} (x_i^2-2x_i\overline{x}+\overline{x}^2) \cdot \frac{f_i}{n}\\ &= \sum_{i=1}^{k} (x_i^2-2x_i\overline{x}+\overline{x}^2) \cdot p_i\\ &= \sum_{i=1}^{k} x_i^2p_i - 2\sum_{i=1}^{k} x_ip_i\overline{x} + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \overline{x}^2p_i\\ &= \sum_{i=1}^{k} x_i^2p_i - 2\overline{x}\sum_{i=1}^{k} x_ip_i + \overline{x}^2\sum_{i=1}^{k} p_i\\ &= \sum_{i=1}^{k} x_i^2p_i - 2\overline{x}^2 + \overline{x}^2\\ &= \sum_{i=1}^{k} x_i^2p_i - \overline{x}^2 \end{align*}

item.title: two dimensional random variable
item.description: the pair \((X,Y)\) is a random variable if \(X\) and \(Y\) are defined over the same probability space refer to joint probability distribution let \((X,Y)\) be a two dimensional random variable, the probability function (called marginal distribution) of \(X\) is defined as \[ P(X=x)=\sum_{y} P(X=x,Y=y) \] and of \(Y\) is defined as \[P(Y=y)=\sum_{x} P(X=x,Y=y)\] let \((X,Y)\) be a two dimensional random variable, the conditional probability function of \(X\) for \(Y=y\) is \[ P(X=x\mid Y=y)=\frac{P(X=x,Y=y)}{P(Y=y)} \] and similarly, \[ P(Y=y\mid X=x)=\frac{P(X=x,Y=y)}{P(X=x)} \] we throw a symmetric coin with the values 0 and 1, if we get 0 we throw a die, if we get 1 we throw the same coin again.

ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) consider a discrete random variable \(X\) that receives the values \(X_1,X_2,\dots\) with the probabilities \(P_1,P_2,\dots\) respectively, the expected value of \(X\), denoted by \(\mathrm{E}(X)\), is defined as \(\mathrm{E}(X)=\sum_{i} X_iP_i\) such that the series is absolutely convergent if \(X\) is a constant random variable, i.e. it receives only one value \(C\) with a probability of 1 then \(\mathrm{E}(X)=C\) if \(a\) is constant, then for each random variable whose expected value is finite \(\mathrm{E}(aX)=a\cdot \mathrm{E}(X)\) let \(X\) and \(Y\) be two random variables defined over the same probability space, then \[ \mathrm{E}(X+Y)=\mathrm{E}(X)+\mathrm{E}(Y) \] let \(X \sim B(n,p)\), we look at \(X\) as the number of successes from a sequence of \(n\) independent trials

item.title: frequency
item.description: the frequency of an event \(i\) is the number \(n_{i}\) of times the observation has occurred/recorded in an experiment or study

item.title: joint distribution table
item.description: in a 2 dimensional table, each cell represents the joint probability of the two variables, i.e. \(P(X=x,Y=y)\) the sum of all the cells in a joint probability table is 1

item.title: joint probability distribution
item.description: given two random variables that are defined on the same probability space, the joint probability distribution is the corresponding probability distribution on all possible pairs of outputs. it is denoted by \(P(X=x,Y=y)\) \[ P(X=x,Y=y) \geq 0 \] \[ \sum_{x} \sum_{y} P(X=x,Y=y)=1 \]

item.title: fixed-length code word
item.description: binary coding \(k\) is fixed-length if for every two characters \(c_j,c_i\), \(|k(c_i)|=|k(c_j)|\)

item.title: probability homework 1
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(
ewcommand\dif[1]{\:	extrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{ceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter loor{\lfloor}{floor}\) \(
ewcommand{\ihat}{\hat{	extbf{i}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\jhat}{\hat{	extbf{j}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) let A,B,C be events, find the probability expressions for the following: only A happens \[ P(A) \cap P\left(\overline{B}ight) \cap P\left(\overline{C}ight) \] atleast one of them happens \[ P(A) \cup P(B) \cup P( C) \] only one of them happens \[ \left(P(A) \cap P(\overline{B}) \cap P(\overline{C})ight) \cup \left(P(\overline{A}) \cap P(B) \cap P(\overline{C})ight) \cup \left(P(\overline{A}) \cap P(\overline{B}) \cap P( C)ight) \] none of the events happen \[ P(\overline{A})+P(\overline{B})+P(\overline{C}) \] no more than 2 events happen 1 minus the probability of all 3 events happening: \[ 1 - P(A) \cap P(B) \cap P( C) \] prove: \[ P(A \cup B \cup C) = P(A)+P(B)+P( C)-P(A \cap B)-P(A \cap C)-P(B \cap C)+P(A \cap B \cap C) \]  egin{align*} P(A \cup B \cup C) &= P(A \cup (B \cup C))\ &= P(A) + P(B \cup C) - P(A \cap (B \cup C))\ &= P(A) + P(B) + P( C) - P(B \cap C) - P(A \cap (B \cup C))\ &= P(A) + P(B) + P( C) - P(B \cap C) - P((A \cap B) \cup (B \cap C))\ &= P(A) + P(B) + P( C) - P(B \cap C) - P(A \cap B) - P(B \cap C) + P((A \cap B) \cap (B \cap C))\ &= P(A) + P(B) + P( C) - P(B \cap C) - P(A \cap B) - P(B \cap C) + P(A \cap B \cap C) \end{align*}

ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) combinatorics is the theory of enumeration, where we look at elements of a set as options rule of sum let \(A,B\) be disjoint finite sets, then \(|A \cup B| = |A| + |B|\) in other words, if the set \(A\) had \(n\) elements (\(n\) options) and \(B\) had \(m\) elements (\(m\) options) such that \(A \cap B = \varnothing\) then there exist \(n+m\) total options to pick from \(A \cup B\) if \(A,B\) are finite sets such that \(A \subseteq B\), then \(|B	extbackslash A| = |B|-|A|\) if a nest had 5 red eggs numbered 1-5 and 3 blue eggs numbered 1-3, how many options do we have if we wanted to pick a single egg?

item.title: asymptotic notation
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(
ewcommand\dif[1]{\:	extrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{ceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter loor{\lfloor}{floor}\) \(
ewcommand{\ihat}{\hat{	extbf{i}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\jhat}{\hat{	extbf{j}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) notation name formal definition limit definition \(f(n) = O(g(n))\) Big Oh \(\exists k > 0 \ \exists n_0 \  orall n > n_0 : \vert f(n) \vert \leq k \cdot g(n)\) \(\lim_{n	o\infty}  rac{\vert f(n) \vert}{g(n)} = c\), where \(c \geq 0\) is a constant \(f(n) = \Omega(g(n))\) Big Omega \(\exists k > 0 \ \exists n_0 \  orall n > n_0 : \vert f(n) \vert \geq k \cdot g(n)\) \(\lim_{n	o\infty}  rac{g(n)}{f(n)} = c\), where \(c \geq 0\) is a constant \(f(n) = \Theta(g(n))\) Big Theta \(\exists k_1 > 0\ \exists k_2 > 0\ \exists n_0\  orall n > n_0 : k_1 \cdot g(n) \leq \vert f(n) \vert \leq k_2 \cdot g(n)\) \(\lim_{n	o\infty}  rac{f(n)}{g(n)} = c\), where \(c > 0\) is a constant 1 Big O Big O of a function \(f\), denoted by \(O(f)\), is an upper asymptotic bound of \(f\) 2 Big Omega Big Omega of a function \(f\), denoted by \(\Omega(f)\), is a lower asymptotic bound of \(f\) 3 Big Theta Big Theta of a function \(f\), denoted by \(\Theta(f)\), is tight bound of \(f\), so it is both Big Omega and Big O 4 order of growth the order of growth of an algorithm is an approximation of the time required to run a computer program as the input size increases, the order of growth ignores the constant factor needed for fixed operations and focuses instead on the operations that increase proportional to input size an order of growth is a set of functions whose asymptotic growth behavior is considered equivalent.

ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) these are my notes on reading the book which covers the science of artificial intelligence extensively 1 solving problems by searching a solution is an action sequence, so search algorithms work by considering various possible action sequences. the possible action sequences starting at the initial state form a search tree with the initial state at the root; the branches are actions and the nodes correspond to states in the state space of the problem an additional data structure could be used to keep track of nodes that have been already visited in the tree to prevent redundant repetitions, demonstrated in the GRAPH-SEARCH algorithm which expands on the TREE-SEARCH algorithm (the new lines are highlighted) 2 machine learning 2.

ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) <&kleppner1973> 1 true physics vector a vector in physics may differ a bit from that of a math vector a “true” physics vector is independant of the coordinate system we pick while a non-true physics vector may be dependant and its values may change when we change the coordinate system 2 position vector the position of an arbitrary point P at \((x,y,z)\) is written as \[ r = (x,y,z) = x\ihat + y\jhat + z\khat \] the position vector isnt a true physics vector, because it doesnt really have a direction since it just represents a position in space and so the position vector of a specific point in space may differ from one coordinate system to another, as we can pick any coordinate system we want and it wouldnt affect the position of a point 3 displacement vector if we move from the point \(x_1,y_1,z_1\) to some new position \(x_2,y_2,z_2\) then the displacement defines a “true” vector \(S\) with coordinates \[ S = (x_2-x_1,\ y_2-y_1,\ z_2-z_1) \] a displacement vector is a true physics vector 4 instantaneous velocity instantaneous velocity, usually called just velocity is a quantity that tells us how fast an object is moving at a single point in time and space the SI unit for acceleration is metre per second (\( rac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{s}}\)) velocity can also be defined as the derivative of position with respect to time, as it is the limit of the average velocity as the time interval approaches zero: \[ v = \lim_{\Delta t 	o 0}  rac{x(t+\Delta t)-x(t)}{\Delta t} =  rac{dx}{dt} \] using notation due to Gottfried Leibniz, who independently invented calculus.

item.title: polar coordinate system
item.description:
\(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{an}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{ng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(
ewcommand\dif[1]{\:	extrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{ceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter loor{\lfloor}{floor}\) \(
ewcommand{\ihat}{\hat{	extbf{i}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\jhat}{\hat{	extbf{j}}}\) \(
ewcommand{\khat}{\hat{	extbf{k}}}\) \(
ewcommand{hat}{\hat{	extbf{r}}}\) \(
ewcommand{	hetahat}{ oldsymbol{\hat{	heta}}}\) a 2d coordinate system points in the \(x,y\) plane are described not by \((x,y)\) coordinates but by \((r,	heta)\) coordinates, where \(r\) is the magnitude and \(	heta\) is the angle between the vector and the \(x\) axis  egin{align*} r &= \sqrt{x^2+y^2}\ 	heta &= \arctan\left( rac{y}{x}ight) \end{align*} the base vectors of this coordinate system are usually denoted by \(hat\) and \(	hetahat\)  egin{align*} hat(	heta) &= \cos	heta\ihat + \sin	heta\jhat\ 	hetahat(	heta) &= -\sin	heta\ihat + \cos	heta\jhat \end{align*}

item.title: average
item.description: the average of a set of numbers is simply the sum of the numbers divided by their count

item.title: rational root theorem
item.description: \(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\ran}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\rng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(\newcommand\dif[1]{\:\textrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{\rceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\floor{\lfloor}{\rfloor}\) the rational root theorem states a constraint on rational solutions of a polynomial \(a_{n}x^{n}+a_{n-1}x^{n-1}+\cdots+a_{0} = 0\) with integer coefficients \(a_{i}\in \mathbb {Z}\) and \(a_{0}, a_{n}\neq 0\) the theorem states that each rational solution \(x = p⁄q\), written in lowest terms so that \(p\) and \(q\) are relatively prime, satisfies: \(p\) is an integer factor of the constant term \(a_0\), and \(q\) is an integer factor of the leading coefficient \(a_n\).

item.title: probability
item.description: \(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\ran}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\rng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(\newcommand\dif[1]{\:\textrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{\rceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\floor{\lfloor}{\rfloor}\) probability is a function, denoted by \(P\) that describes the probability for a specific incident to occur The probability of an impossible event is zero; the probability of a certain event is one. therefore, for any event \(A\), the range of possible probabilities is \(0 \le P(A) \le 1\) for \(S\) the sample space of all possibilities, \(P(S) = 1\). that is the sum of all the probabilities for all possible events is equal to one.

item.title: discrete math
item.description: \(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\ran}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\rng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(\newcommand\dif[1]{\:\textrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{\rceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\floor{\lfloor}{\rfloor}\) 1 set 1.1 disjoint sets two sets are said to be disjoint sets if they have no element in common 1.2 multiset a multiset is a modification of the concept of a set that, unlike a set, allows for multiple instances for each of its elements. the number of instances given for each element is called the multiplicity of that element in the multiset 2 ordered pair given \(a, b\) are mathematical objects, \((a,b)\) is an ordered pair, \((a,b) \neq (b,a)\), as an example, the \((x,y)\) coordinates on a function is an ordered pair 3 cartesian product cartesian product of 2 sets is the set of all the possible ordered pairs that can be obtained by taking an element from \(A\) as the first in the pair and an element from \(B\) as the second \begin{gather*} A \times B = \{(a,b) \ | \ a \in A \land b \in B\}\\ (a,b) \in A \times B \Leftrightarrow a \in A \land b \in B \end{gather*}

item.title: Arithmetic progression
item.description: \(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\ran}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\rng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(\newcommand\dif[1]{\:\textrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{\rceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\floor{\lfloor}{\rfloor}\) An arithmetic progression or arithmetic sequence is a sequence of numbers such that the difference between the consecutive terms is constant if the first number of an arithmetic progression is \(a_{1}\) and the difference of successive numbers is \(d\) then the formula for the $n$th number in the sequence is: \[ a_{n} = a_1 + (n-1)d \] and the general formula is \[ a_{n} = a_m + (n-m)d \] where \(a_m\) is a number in the sequence at index \(m\)

item.title: Euler's Number
item.description: \(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\ran}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\rng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(\newcommand\dif[1]{\:\textrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{\rceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\floor{\lfloor}{\rfloor}\) Euler’s Number is a constant defined as \[ e = \lim_{x\to \pm\infty} \left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right)^x \]

item.title: geometric progression
item.description: \(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\ran}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\rng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(\newcommand\dif[1]{\:\textrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{\rceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\floor{\lfloor}{\rfloor}\) a geometric progression is a sequence of non-zero numbers where each term after the first is found by multiplying the previous one by a fixed, non-zero number called the common ratio the formula for the $n$th number in a geometric sequence with initial value \(a\) and common ratio \(r\) is: \[ a_{n}=ar^{n-1} \]

item.title: sum of arithmetic sequence
item.description: \(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\ran}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\rng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(\newcommand\dif[1]{\:\textrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{\rceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\floor{\lfloor}{\rfloor}\) the sum of the numbers in a finite Arithmetic sequence is given by the following formula: \[ S = \frac{n(a_1 + a_n)}{2} \]

item.title: sum of geometric progression
item.description: \(\DeclareMathOperator{\spn}{span}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\dom}{domain}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\ran}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\rng}{range}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\img}{Im}\) \(\DeclareMathOperator{\adj}{adj}\) \(\newcommand\dif[1]{\:\textrm{d}#1}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\ceil{\lceil}{\rceil}\) \(\DeclarePairedDelimiter\floor{\lfloor}{\rfloor}\) the sum of a finite geometric progression with initial term \(a\), common ratio \(r\) and \(n\) terms can be found using the formula: \[ S_n = \frac{a(1-r^n)}{1-r} \]
